event: Austronesian voyagers, descendants of the Lapita cultural migration out of Southeast Asia, begin settling the atolls of the Marshall Islands.
Permanent communities take hold across the coral atolls, developing societies organized around clan land rights and chiefly authority.
Marshallese navigators perfect sophisticated stick charts mapping ocean swells and wave patterns to guide canoe voyages between distant atolls.
The Treaty of Tordesillas nominally places the region within the Spanish hemisphere, foreshadowing later European claims.
Spanish navigator Alonso de Salazar makes the first recorded European sighting of an atoll in the group.
Alvaro de Saavedra and other Spanish explorers chart further islands while crossing the Pacific.
British captains John Marshall and Thomas Gilbert sail through the islands, and the group is subsequently named the Marshall Islands after Marshall.
Russian explorer Otto von Kotzebue conducts surveys of the atolls during his Pacific expeditions.
Protestant missionaries from the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions arrive and begin Christianizing the population.
Germany signs a treaty securing a coaling and trading station at Jaluit, expanding its commercial foothold.
Germany formally annexes the Marshall Islands as a protectorate, administering them through the Jaluit Company.
Administration of the islands is transferred to German New Guinea as copra exports dominate the colonial economy.
Japan seizes the Marshall Islands from Germany at the outbreak of World War I.
The League of Nations grants Japan a mandate over the islands, which are governed as part of the South Seas Mandate.
United States forces capture Kwajalein and Enewetak from Japan in major World War II battles, ending Japanese control.
The United States relocates the people of Bikini Atoll and begins atomic testing there with Operation Crossroads.
The islands become part of the United Nations Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands under United States administration.
The population of Enewetak Atoll is removed as the United States establishes a second nuclear test site.
The massive Castle Bravo hydrogen bomb test at Bikini showers Rongelap and other inhabited atolls with radioactive fallout.
The United States ends atmospheric nuclear testing in the Marshalls after detonating 67 nuclear devices since 1946.
The Congress of Micronesia is established, giving Marshallese and other islanders a representative legislative body.
The Republic of the Marshall Islands adopts its constitution and establishes self-government with Amata Kabua as first president.
Rongelap islanders, suffering ongoing radiation-related illness, petition for relocation away from their contaminated home atoll.
Marshallese voters approve the Compact of Free Association with the United States in a plebiscite.
Greenpeace's Rainbow Warrior evacuates the people of Rongelap to Mejatto Island amid lingering radiation fears.
The Compact of Free Association enters into force, granting the Marshall Islands sovereignty in free association with the United States.
A Nuclear Claims Tribunal is established to adjudicate compensation for damage and injury from the testing program.
The United Nations Security Council formally terminates the Trust Territory status for the Marshall Islands.
The Marshall Islands is admitted to the United Nations as a full member state.
Founding president Amata Kabua dies in office after leading the nation since its constitutional establishment.
The Marshall Islands establishes diplomatic relations with Taiwan, a partnership it has maintained since.
An amended Compact of Free Association with the United States is signed, extending economic assistance for twenty years.
Severe king tides and flooding inundate parts of Majuro, highlighting the threat of rising seas to the low-lying nation.
A state of emergency is declared after a prolonged drought caused by shifting climate patterns strikes the northern atolls.
The Marshall Islands files lawsuits against nuclear-armed states at the International Court of Justice over disarmament obligations.
Hilda Heine is elected the first female president of the Marshall Islands and of any independent Pacific nation.
Parliament approves the Sovereign, a national digital cryptocurrency intended to circulate alongside the US dollar.
The Marshall Islands largely keeps COVID-19 at bay through strict border controls before recording its first local cases later.
The Marshall Islands and the United States sign a renewed twenty-year Compact agreement, including a new nuclear legacy trust fund.
Hilda Heine returns to the presidency as the nation continues to press for climate adaptation funding and nuclear justice.
